schools.summary: Abimelech going to Shechem to scheme with his mother's kindred: 'and Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying' — Abimelech, Gideon's son by a concubine, went to plot with his mother's kin (the and Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, Abimelech to Shechem, the and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying, the plot begun, the figure, the ambitious son scheming with his kindred for the kingdom, the truth that Abimelech sought the throne by the help of his mother's people at Shechem, Judges 8:31, the figure of the ambition that schemes for power, the kindred enlisted, the plot of Abimelech, the conspiracy begun, vv.31); Abimelech going to his mother's kin (the plot, Judges 8:31) — the going (to Shechem); 'communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father' (Abimelech scheming with the kindred, the plot, the figure, the ambition that schemes for power, the providence, the plot, the appeal to the men of Shechem to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech's appeal to the men of Shechem, pleading his kinship: 'speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you? remember also that I am your bone and your flesh' — Abimelech urged the Shechemites to prefer him, their kinsman, to the seventy (the speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you?, the false alternative, the remember also that I am your bone and your flesh, the plea of kinship, the figure, the demagogue's appeal, the false choice and the plea of blood, the truth that Abimelech set a false alternative and pleaded kinship to win the throne, Genesis 29:14, the figure of the ambition that flatters and deceives the people to gain power, the appeal of Abimelech, the plea of kinship, vv.1); Abimelech setting a false alternative (the demagoguery) — the choice (one or seventy); 'remember also that I am your bone and your flesh' (the plea of kinship to win them, the appeal, Genesis 29:14, the figure, the ambition that flatters the people, the providence, the appeal, the men of Shechem inclining after Abimelech to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

Abimelech setting a false alternative and pleading kinship to win the throne
the ambition that flatters and deceives the people to gain power (Genesis 29:14)

schools.summary: The Shechemites inclining to follow Abimelech as their brother: 'and his mother's brethren spake of him in the ears of all the men of Shechem all these words: and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother' — the kindred swayed the men, whose hearts turned to Abimelech as their kinsman (the and his mother's brethren spake of him in the ears of all the men of Shechem all these words, the kindred spoke, the and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother, their hearts inclined, the figure, the people swayed by kinship rather than right, the heart turned by partiality, the truth that the Shechemites chose Abimelech for blood, not for worth, the heart inclined by partiality, Judges 9:2, the figure of the people swayed by self-interest and kinship to a wrong choice, the inclining of the people, the choice for Abimelech, vv.2); the kindred swaying the men (the persuasion, Judges 9:2) — the speaking (the kindred spoke); 'their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother' (the hearts inclined by kinship, the partiality, the figure, the people swayed by kinship to a wrong choice, the providence, the inclining, the money given to hire vain men to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech hiring vain men with silver from Baal's temple: 'and they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him' — Abimelech hired worthless men with money from the idol's temple (the and they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, silver from Baal's house, the wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him, vain men hired, the figure, the idol's money buying worthless men for the bloody work, the truth that the wages of the idol-temple bought a band of reckless men, the means as base as the end, Judges 8:33, the figure of the ungodly enterprise furnished by ungodly means, the worthless hired for evil, the hire of Abimelech, the band of vain men, vv.3); the silver from Baal's temple (the means, Judges 8:33) — the silver (from Baalberith); 'Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him' (the worthless men hired for the work, the band, the figure, the ungodly enterprise furnished by ungodly means, the providence, the hire, the slaughter of the seventy to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him

schools.summary: Abimelech slaying his seventy brethren upon one stone, only Jotham escaping: 'and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself' — the bloodshed of ambition — 'and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left': Abimelech butchered his seventy brothers on a single stone to clear his path to the throne (the witnesses noting the significance — the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the murder of seventy brethren, the sons of the deliverer Gideon, slaughtered like victims on one stone to secure a usurped crown, the basest treachery and cruelty in the pursuit of power, the seventy slain by the hired band, the deliberate butchery on one stone, the deepest ingratitude against Gideon's house and the basest crime against kindred, the lesson — that ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becomes murderous, trampling every bond of nature and blood to grasp at power, 'they that will be rich fall into... many foolish and hurtful lusts,' 1 Timothy 6:9-10, the truth that the love of power, like the love of money, is a root of all evil, leading men to the foulest crimes, 'from whence come wars and fightings... ye lust, and have not: ye kill,' James 4:1-2, the figure — the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves, the type of every tyrant and of the spirit that murdered the heirs to seize the inheritance, 'this is the heir; come, let us kill him,' Matthew 21:38, the truth that God marks such blood and will require it, the cry of the seventy not unheard, the figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred, the call to fear the God who avenges innocent blood, the warning against the ambition that destroys all bonds to grasp at power, the figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred); Abimelech butchering his seventy brothers on one stone to clear his path to the throne, the deliberate murder of Gideon's house (the bloodshed of ambition, 1 Timothy 6:9-10) — the slaughter (seventy on one stone); 'notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself' (Jotham alone escaping by hiding, God preserving one to pronounce the curse, the love of power leading to the foulest crime yet not utterly prevailing, the lust that kills, James 4:1-2; Matthew 21:38) — the figure (the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves, the providence, the murder, Abimelech made king at Shechem to follow, vv.6) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Abimelech's slaughter of the seventy signify?

sides.position: The murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred - the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the seventy butchered on one stone for a usurped crown, ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becoming murderous (1 Timothy 6:9-10), the love of power a root of foulest crime (James 4:1-2), a type of the spirit that murders the heirs to seize the inheritance (Matthew 21:38)

themes: And he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself

the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the seventy sons of the deliverer slaughtered on one stone to secure a usurped crown, the basest treachery and cruelty in the pursuit of power

ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becoming murderous, a type of the spirit that murders the heirs to seize the inheritance, the blood God will require (1 Timothy 6:9-10; James 4:1-2; Matthew 21:38)

schools.summary: The Shechemites making Abimelech king: 'and all the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem' — the Shechemites crowned Abimelech, the fruit of the bloody plot (the and all the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, the men gathered, the and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem, Abimelech made king, the figure, the usurper crowned by the people, the throne built on blood, the truth that the Shechemites set up Abimelech as king, the murder bearing its fruit, Judges 8:23, the figure of the kingdom usurped by violence and confirmed by the people's folly, the crowning of Abimelech, the throne of blood, vv.5); the men of Shechem and Millo gathered (the assembly, Judges 8:23) — the gathering (the men); 'made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem' (Abimelech crowned king, the usurpation, the figure, the kingdom usurped by violence, the providence, the crowning, Jotham's parable from mount Gerizim to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And all the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem

schools.summary: Jotham crying out to the men of Shechem from mount Gerizim: 'and when they told it to Jotham, he went and stood in the top of mount Gerizim, and lifted up his voice, and cried, and said unto them, Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you' — Jotham, the spared son, cried to the Shechemites from the mount (the and when they told it to Jotham, he went and stood in the top of mount Gerizim, and lifted up his voice, and cried, Jotham cried, the and said unto them, Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you, his call to hear, the figure, the lone surviving son crying the truth to the guilty people from the mount of blessing, the truth that Jotham boldly rebuked the Shechemites, that they hear or God hear against them, Deuteronomy 11:29, the figure of the prophet's voice that calls the guilty to hear, the appeal to God's hearing, the cry of Jotham, the call to hear, vv.5); Jotham crying from mount Gerizim (the appeal, Deuteronomy 11:29) — the cry (from Gerizim); 'Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you' (the call to hear lest God hear against them, the appeal, the figure, the prophet's voice that calls the guilty to hear, the providence, the cry, the parable of the trees to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham's parable of the trees going forth to anoint a king, asking the olive first: 'the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us' — the parable's design — 'the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us': Jotham frames a fable in which the trees seek a king, first asking the olive (the witnesses noting the significance — the most ancient of parables, framed to expose the folly of the Shechemites in making Abimelech king, the trees a figure of the people seeking a ruler, the worthy and useful trees (the olive, fig, and vine) one by one declining the honour, while only the worthless bramble accepts, the fable holding up a mirror to Shechem, the people's restless desire for a king, the better men unwilling to leave their useful callings for empty rule, the basest grasping at the power the best refuse, the lesson — that those most fit to rule are often least eager for it, content in their fruitful service, while the ambitious and worthless press forward, 'seekest thou great things for thyself? seek them not,' Jeremiah 45:5, the truth that true greatness lies in fruitful service rather than in lordship, and that the desire to rule is no mark of worth, 'whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister,' Matthew 20:26, the figure — the wisdom that exposes the vanity of ambition and the folly of a people who exalt the unworthy, the parable a glass for every generation that prefers the bramble to the olive, the truth that God's true servants seek not their own exaltation but their appointed fruitfulness, the figure of the folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp, the call to be content in fruitful service rather than to grasp at power, the warning that a people who crown the bramble reap its fire, the figure of the folly of seeking a ruler, the worthy declining what the worthless grasp); Jotham framing a fable of the trees seeking a king, the oldest of parables, a mirror held up to the folly of Shechem (the parable's design, Jeremiah 45:5) — the fable (the trees seek a king); 'and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us' (the trees asking the olive, the most useful, to reign, the worthy and fruitful sought first, the better men least eager for empty rule, true greatness in fruitful service, Matthew 20:26) — the figure (the wisdom that exposes the vanity of ambition, the providence, the parable, the olive's refusal to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Jotham's parable of the trees seeking a king teach?

sides.position: The folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp - the oldest of parables exposing the folly of Shechem in crowning Abimelech, the worthy trees declining the honour while only the bramble accepts, those most fit to rule least eager for it and content in fruitful service (Jeremiah 45:5), true greatness in service rather than lordship (Matthew 20:26)

themes: The trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us

the most ancient of parables, framed to expose the folly of Shechem in crowning Abimelech, the worthy and useful trees declining the honour while only the bramble accepts
those most fit to rule least eager for it and content in fruitful service while the worthless press forward, true greatness in service rather than lordship (Jeremiah 45:5; Matthew 20:26)

schools.summary: The olive declining to reign, unwilling to leave its fatness: 'but the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?' — the olive refused, unwilling to leave its useful fatness for rule (the but the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, the olive's refusal, the wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?, its useful service, the figure, the olive content with its fruitful service, declining the empty honour of rule, the truth that the olive, useful to God and man, would not forsake its fatness for lordship, Psalm 104:15, the figure of the worthy who prefer fruitful service to empty promotion, the useful declining the throne, the olive's refusal, the fruitful service preferred, vv.8); the olive declining to leave its fatness (the refusal, Psalm 104:15) — the refusal (leave my fatness); 'wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?' (the olive's fruitful service honouring God and man, the useful calling, the figure, the worthy who prefer fruitful service to empty promotion, the providence, the refusal, the fig declining likewise to follow, vv.10-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The trees asking the fig tree to reign: 'and the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us' — the trees turned next to the fig tree (the and the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us, the fig asked, the figure, the second worthy tree sought, the fruitful again offered the rule, the truth that the trees, refused by the olive, asked the fig, another useful tree, Judges 9:8, the figure of the worthy again sought and again to decline, the fruitful preferring service, the offer to the fig, the second refusal to come, vv.8); the trees turning to the fig (the offer, Judges 9:8) — the offer (to the fig); 'Come thou, and reign over us' (the fig asked to reign, the second offer, the figure, the worthy again sought to decline, the providence, the offer, the fig's refusal to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The fig declining to reign, unwilling to leave its sweetness: 'but the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to be promoted over the trees?' — the fig refused, unwilling to leave its sweetness for rule (the but the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, the fig's refusal, the and go to be promoted over the trees?, the rule declined, the figure, the fig content with its sweet fruit, declining the empty honour of rule, the truth that the fig, like the olive, would not forsake its good fruit for lordship, Luke 13:6, the figure of the worthy who prefer their fruitful calling to empty promotion, the fig's refusal, the sweet fruit preferred, vv.9); the fig declining to leave its sweetness (the refusal, Luke 13:6) — the refusal (forsake my sweetness); 'go to be promoted over the trees?' schools.summary: The trees asking the vine to reign: 'then said the trees unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us' — the trees turned next to the vine (the then said the trees unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us, the vine asked, the figure, the third worthy tree sought, the fruitful again offered the rule, the truth that the trees, refused twice, asked the vine, another useful tree, Judges 9:8, the figure of the worthy again sought and again to decline, the fruitful preferring service, the offer to the vine, the third offer to reign, vv.8); the trees turning to the vine (the offer, Judges 9:8) — the offer (to the vine); 'Come thou, and reign over us' (the vine asked to reign, the third offer, the figure, the worthy again sought to decline, the providence, the offer, the vine's refusal to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The vine declining to reign, unwilling to leave its wine that cheers God and man: 'and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?' — the fruitful service preferred — 'and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?': the vine, like the olive and fig, refuses the rule, unwilling to forsake its wine (the witnesses noting the significance — the third and crowning refusal of the worthy, the vine prizing its God-given fruitfulness, the wine that gladdens God in the drink offering and man in his joy, above the empty and burdensome honour of lordship, the best of the trees content in their useful callings and unwilling to leave them for rule, the wine that cheers God, used in the offerings, and man, in his gladness, the vine's service better than a throne, the worthy declining what the worthless will grasp, the lesson — that the true servant of God prizes his appointed fruitfulness above place and power, content to bless God and man in his calling rather than to be exalted, 'not slothful in business; fervent in spirit; serving the Lord,' Romans 12:11, the truth that to leave a fruitful service for empty honour is no gain but loss, the useful life better than the lofty seat, 'godliness with contentment is great gain,' 1 Timothy 6:6, the figure — the wine that cheers God and man a figure of the gladness God's true servants bring, fulfilled in the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man, 'I am the true vine,' John 15:1, the truth that the worthy seek not promotion but fruitfulness, and that the desire to rule is no mark of grace, the figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule, the call to be content and fruitful in our callings, the warning that to grasp at rule is to leave the better part, the figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule); 'and go to be promoted over the trees?' (the vine declining the empty honour of rule, the useful life better than the lofty seat, the worthy prizing fruitfulness above place, a figure of the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man, the contentment in service, 1 Timothy 6:6; John 15:1) — the figure (the wine that cheers God and man, the gladness God's servants bring, the providence, the refusal, the bramble accepting the rule to follow, vv.14-15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the vine's refusal to leave its wine teach?

sides.position: The worthy prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule - the vine prizing its wine that gladdens God and man above lordship, the true servant prizing his appointed fruitfulness above place and power (Romans 12:11), godliness with contentment great gain (1 Timothy 6:6), a figure of the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man (John 15:1)
